prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 27-year-old Black man.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I had a urine test after my annual physical. I ran a half-marathon the day before the test. I feel completely fine--no pain with urinating, no back pain, no fever, and no visible blood in the urine.
 - History: No known kidney problems.
 - Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 64
 - Exam: No exam or imaging done.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Urinalysis RBC: 8-10 /hpf (ref 0-2) HIGH
 - Urinalysis blood: Moderate (ref Negative) ABNORMAL
 - Urinalysis WBC: 0-2 /hpf (ref 0-5) normal
 - Urinalysis protein: Negative (ref Negative) normal
 - Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL (0.7-1.3) normal
 
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your urine test shows microscopic blood (8–10 RBCs/hpf, “moderate” blood) without signs of infection (normal WBC) and without protein, and your kidney function (creatinine) is normal. Because you ran a half‑marathon the day before, a common benign explanation is exercise‑induced hematuria, which often resolves on its own within a few days. However, any confirmed blood in urine should be rechecked when you’re well‑hydrated and have avoided strenuous exercise for 48–72 hours. If it persists on repeat testing, your doctor may evaluate other causes (stones, structural issues, less commonly kidney disease). Seek urgent care sooner if you develop visible blood, pain, fever, or urinary symptoms.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your urine test shows microscopic blood (8–10 RBCs/hpf and “moderate” blood on dipstick) without signs of infection (normal WBC) and without kidney function impairment (normal creatinine) or protein leakage (negative protein). Because you ran a half-marathon the day before, this can be exercise‑associated hematuria, which often resolves on its own after rest and hydration. However, any confirmed microscopic hematuria still needs follow‑up to make sure it clears and to rule out other causes. The usual next step is repeating a urinalysis after avoiding strenuous exercise for a few days (often 48–72 hours) and confirming whether blood persists.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your urine test shows microscopic blood (8–10 red blood cells per high-power field and “moderate” blood on dipstick) without signs of infection (normal WBC), without protein in the urine, and with normal kidney function (creatinine 0.9). Running a half-marathon the day before can cause temporary blood in the urine from exercise-related irritation/trauma and dehydration, even when you feel well. Because you have no pain, fever, visible blood, or abnormal kidney labs, this is not an emergency based on the information provided. The usual next step is to repeat a urinalysis after avoiding strenuous exercise for several days to see if it resolves; if it persists, your doctor would evaluate other causes.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 78%

Because you ran a half-marathon the day before and you feel well, this pattern can happen from exercise-related microscopic hematuria and often resolves on its own. However, blood in the urine should still be confirmed and rechecked when you haven’t exercised for a couple of days, because persistent hematuria can come from other causes (stones, structural issues, kidney/urinary tract problems). A repeat urinalysis is typically the next step.

Running a half-marathon the day before can cause temporary blood in the urine (“exercise-induced hematuria”), which often resolves after 24–72 hours of rest and hydration. Even when you feel fine, persistent microscopic blood should be rechecked because it can also come from stones, urinary tract irritation, or (less likely at your age) other urinary tract conditions. The usual next step is repeating a urinalysis after avoiding strenuous exercise for a few days.

Because you have no pain, fever, visible blood, or abnormal kidney labs, this is unlikely to be an emergency.
